_about: Synthesis of what each pip NUMBER (Ace–Ten) came to mean across suits and decks. Authored from the deck grammars; the per-number evidence (every reading linked to <deck>#<item>) is regenerated by scripts/analyze_numbers.py into _numbers-evidence.json. Keyed by number 1–10.
_intro: For roughly three centuries the numbered cards meant nothing in particular: a Four was four coins, a quantity to be counted in a game. The meaning came late and all at once. The Golden Dawn's *Book T* (1888) laid the ten pips of each suit over the ten **Sephiroth** of the Kabbalistic Tree of Life — Ace to Kether, Ten to Malkuth — so that the *number* now fixed a meaning and the *suit* only coloured it with its element. This is why, reading the grammars across all four suits, the Five is the card of trouble *everywhere* (Strife, Material Trouble, Loss in Pleasure, Defeat) and the Six the card of success *everywhere* (Victory, Material Success, Pleasure, Earned Success): it is the number doing the work, not the suit. The scheme is strikingly self-consistent — and it is a deliberate occult imposition of the 1880s, not an ancient secret. It is perhaps the single clearest demonstration of this book's whole argument: that the meaning was *added*, in a particular place, by particular hands, to pictures that had been mere tallies before.
1: **Ace · Kether — the root.** The Golden Dawn calls each Ace the *Root of the Powers* of its element: pure potential before anything has happened to it — the seed of fire (will), water (love), air (mind), earth (matter). Across every suit the Ace is the same gesture, a single hand offering the element whole and undivided. The number says *origin*; the suit says *of what*.
2: **Two · Chokmah — first force.** With the Two the element is set in motion and directed outward: Dominion in Batons, Love in Cups, Peace-restored in Swords, Harmonious Change in Coins. The shared note is a polarity held in balance — two things in relation — the first stirring of will out of the Ace's potential.
3: **Three · Binah — first fruition.** The Two's impulse takes form and yields a first result: Established Strength, Abundance, Material Works — and, in the cutting suit of Swords, Sorrow. Three is the number of something brought into being, for joy or for grief; the element has produced its first concrete fruit.
4: **Four · Chesed — stability.** Here the suit consolidates and rests: Perfected Work, Earthly Power, Rest from Strife, Blended Pleasure. Every Four is a holding, a settling, a gain secured — even the Cups' weary 'enough' is satiety, a stability gone slightly stale. This is the answer to the question 'does the Four mean the same across the suits?': yes — it means *consolidation*, coloured by what is being consolidated.
5: **Five · Geburah — severity.** The stable Four is broken open. The Five is the bad-news number in every suit at once: Strife, Material Trouble, Loss in Pleasure, Defeat. Severity, conflict, the friction that sharpens or the loss that hollows — the number's meaning is so consistent that the suit only chooses the *flavour* of the trouble.
6: **Six · Tiphareth — harmony restored.** After the Five's break, balance returns and is shared: Victory, Material Success, Pleasure, Earned Success. The Six is the good-news number across the board — success that is recognised, given, remembered, or earned. Harmony at the centre of the Tree, mirrored in all four suits.
7: **Seven · Netzach — desire under strain.** The Six's harmony is tested by appetite and doubt: Valour against the odds, Success Unfulfilled, Illusionary Success, Unstable Effort. Every Seven is a wavering — holding on, waiting, or being deceived — effort that has not yet resolved into a result.
8: **Eight · Hod — activity and intellect.** Motion and sorting: Swiftness, Prudence, Shortened Force, Abandoned Success. The Eight is the element in busy, analytical movement — rushing, refining, restricting, or walking away — the mind's quick work upon the suit's matter.
9: **Nine · Yesod — intensification near the end.** The suit gathers to its near-final pitch, for better and worse: Great Strength, Material Gain, Material Happiness (the 'wish' card) — and, in Swords, Despair. Nine is almost-complete, the feeling at its strongest just before the close.
10: **Ten · Malkuth — completion in the world.** The element comes fully to ground, its end-state made manifest: Wealth, Perfected Success — and, where the suit's nature is hard, Oppression and Ruin. The Ten is the suit finished and embodied; whether that completion is a blessing or a burden depends on what was being completed.
